article.body_preview:
DHAKA, June 5 -- Cash-strapped Bangladesh shut its biggest power plant today because it was unable to afford the coal to fuel it, as a sweltering heatwave creates surging electricity demand.

The 1,320-megawatt government-run Payra Power Plant in southern Bangladesh had already slashed production last month due to fuel shortages, but it was forced to go a step further today, manager Shah Abdul Mawla told AFP.

"Due to the coal shortage, the plant came to a complete shutdown at 12.15pm today," Ma

llm_connections.evidence_quote: had already slashed production last month due to fuel shortages
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article explicitly states the Payra power plant 'slashed production' and later shut down, which directly describes the facility's output levels matching the Production definition.

llm_connections.evidence_quote: Due to the coal shortage, the plant came to a complete shutdown at 12.15pm today
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article describes a facility-level 'complete shutdown' caused by coal shortage, which is an operational disruption consistent with the Factory_Issue definition.

llm_connections.evidence_quote: The Bangladeshi taka depreciated about 25 per cent against the US dollar last year, driving up the cost of fuel imports and power utilities.
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article explicitly links a 25% depreciation of the taka to higher fuel import costs, matching the feature's focus on exchange-rate movements affecting import costs.

llm_connections.evidence_quote: he hoped production could be restored within three weeks when coal shipments arrived
llm_connections.scope_justification: The article ties the arrival of coal shipments (a logistics event) to restoring plant production, which falls under Delivery's scope of logistics performance and shipment outcomes.
